State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
: Ted Prim 
Date 
Oct. 26, 1978 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Natalie West 
Subject: Effective date of recent s 
amendments 
Recent legislative amendments take effect January 1, 
979. 
Questions have been raised concerning the applicability of the 
newly enacted standards to statements of economic interests which 
are filed after January 1, 1979, but which disclose transactions 
occurring during 1978. 
The staff bel 
s that policy considerations support the 
application of amendments to filings occurring after January I, 
1979, except Section 87313 which imposes a disclosure obligation 
on persons making gi 
s on behalf of another or while acting as 
an agent or intermediary of another. 
All of the other changes 
lessen the amount of disclosure required and will reduce the 
burden on individual filers. 
The Commission requested 
staff to research the legal 
principles applicable to retroactive application of a statute. 
Based on my research, I nave conc 
that the cases do not 
provide clear guidance 
it is unlikely that 
could 
successfully challenge the retroactive appl 
statutory requirement. 
The general rule of statutory construct 
is 
a 
statute can have retroactive ef 
t

extent pro-
hibited by state and 
1 constitutional 
but that 
a statute is not retroactive unless the Legislature has expressly 
so 
laredo 
Balen v. Peralta Junior College District, 11 Cal. 
3d 821 (1974). 
However, the California Supreme Court has really 
stretched the concept of

At the same time, however, the

sections.conclusion: The staff believes that policy considerations support the application of amendments to filings occurring after January 1, 1979, except Section 87313 which imposes a disclosure obligation on persons making gifts on behalf of another or while acting as an agent or intermediary of another. All of the other changes lessen the amount of disclosure required and will reduce the burden on individual filers.
sections.question_synthetic: Should recent legislative amendments to FPPC requirements that take effect January 1, 1979 apply retroactively to statements of economic interest filed after that date but disclosing 1978 transactions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC staff concludes that the legislative amendments should apply retroactively to filings after January 1, 1979 (except Section 87313), as this reduces disclosure burdens and legal precedent does not clearly prohibit such retroactive application.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is OCR-scanned with significant text degradation, particularly in latter half. Some passages are illegible or partially corrupted (e.g., 'bel s', 'gi s', 'conc', 'appl'). Question and conclusion sections are identifiable but some supporting legal analysis text is unclear due to OCR quality.

CONCLUSION: The staff believes that policy considerations support the application of amendments to filings occurring after January 1, 1979, except Section 87313 which imposes a disclosure obligation on persons making gifts on behalf of another or while acting as an agent or intermediary of another. All of the other changes lessen the amount of disclosure required and will reduce the burden on individual filers.

embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum addressing whether recent legislative amendments effective January 1, 1979 should apply retroactively to statements of economic interest filed after that date disclosing 1978 transactions. Staff recommends retroactive application of most amendments except Section 87313, supported by statutory construction principles and California case law.
